Short answer: it's complicated.

Long answer: GDCM returns the byte buffer as `char*` in c++. This is returned as a `str` by the Python wrappers generated by SWIG. 

On Python 2 `str` is a byte buffer by default and everything is OK. On Python 3, `str` is a unicode string and no longer a byte buffer. Since we want to access the bytes directly we need to re-encode the unicode string to their original corresponding bytes (a `bytearray` in py3).

This requires us to know the original encoding of the file, which we can only guess, as far as I am aware. This is why we need to try a bunch of codecs. Ideally we would be able to slecet the appropriate codec directly, but I don't know how to do that.

We also need to specify the special surrogate error handler since some bytes have no corresponding unicode character and this would result in an exception under normal conditions.

Also see the SWIG docs on this topic:
http://www.swig.org/Doc3.0/Python.html#Python_nn77

This article also explains a lot about unicode handling in py3:
http://python-notes.curiousefficiency.org/en/latest/python3/text_file_processing.html

So SBP_PACK_START and SBP_PACK_END would make this look a little cleaner:

```c
#ifdef TOOLCHAIN_PRAGMA_PACK
#pragma pack(1) // or #pragma pack() for the end of the file
#endif
```

Also, using `SBP_PACKED` so it's a little shorter (hopefully less aesthetically offensive) and added the `SBP_` prefix to make it clear it's a macro we're defining/using and will prevent it from colliding with other names (however unlikely that may be) ... we could even make it lowercase `__sbp_attribute_packed__` to make it look more like `__attribute__((packed))`, if we're so inclined.  Anyway, regardless of aesthetics, if we can make it more explicit what compilers we're supporting with the above approach, then I'm fine with this.

For CE, the stats are not normalized, but the `get()` calls are.  The update rule is:
```
prob = pred[numpy.arange(label.shape[0]), numpy.int64(label)]
self.sum_metric += (-numpy.log(prob + self.eps)).sum()
self.num_inst += label.shape[0]
```
A call to `get_name_value()` returns `sum_metric / num_inst`.

For SCE with loss normalization, the values summed for `sum_metric` are already divided by batch instances as part of the loss calculation, so we just use `num_inst += 1`.

Hi,

The use case is when we have multiple Amazon EKS clusters in the same region and each cluster has its nodegroup with some spot instances in it. Whenever any single spot instance is about to terminate, the cloudWatch event rule will receive a signal for "Spot instance interruption warning" and this rule has a Lambda target to handle the notification from all spot instances from multiple Amazon EKS nodegroup.

The Lambda function will:
1) get the instance ID(i.e. i-xxxxxxxxx) from the notification from CloudWatch Event
2) look up the tags of the instance to get the Amazon EKS cluster name it belongs to (see [#1](https://github.com/pahud/eks-lambda-drainer/issues/1))
3) execute [eks:DescribeCluster](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/eks/describe-cluster.html) to get its endpoint
4) use [client-go](https://github.com/kubernetes/client-go) to visit this Kubernetes API endpoint and perform the pod draining or taint-based eviction via kubernetes API

As this scenario may  have multiple Amazon EKS clusters yet only single Lambda function handling all the spot instances termination, we can't scope it down to a specific single Amazon EKS cluster.

I have made this in [pahud/eks-lambda-drainer](https://github.com/pahud/eks-lambda-drainer) and is about to publish to Serverless App Repo and hopefully everyone can deploy this feature with single button click to deploy it from SAR.

Hi, nomenclature question for you:

One thing that's confused me a bit is the use of the name "callback".

I normally think of callbacks some piece of code that runs asynchronously after some other code is done, particularly in an event loop. I usually expect that a callback will _not return anything_. E.g. typically I've seen something like:

```python
def longRunningAsyncMethod(param, callback):
    result = doSomethingBig(param)
    callback(result)
```

However, here are some examples from the code base:

```python
    for callback in hooks.get_callables(hooks.NAVIGATION_POPULATE):
        for item in callback():
            yield item
```

```python
    for callback in hooks.get_callables(hooks.FRONTEND_PLUGINS):
        bundle_path, stats_file, async_events = callback()
```

```python
    tags = [render_as_async(callback()) for callback in hooks.get_callables(hook)]
    return mark_safe('\n'.join(tags))
```

They all return some value and are used synchronously by something else. Are they more "getter methods" than "callbacks", perhaps?
